PARANOIA 
Here’s some advice for how to get the best Use our ofthe time you've got available to play. 
Creating Characters For beginning players, rolling up a character and filling out a character sheet can be an in volved and puzzling procedure. You can make ita fot less intimidating by talking them through the process. This can be done in 15 to 20 minutes — less if your players have read through the Player section, Tt might be even better to give your players 
ppregenerated characters. We include a set with each adventure we publish, and you can always rall up a bunch of characters on your ‘own and hand the filledsin sheets to your players. {Using pregenerated characters is'a good idea for two reasons: First, Paranoia characters need personalities that fit the peculiar Paranoia universe, We tailor the abilities and idiosyncrasies of our pregenerated characters to fit the adventure, ‘We design the characters as a team, antici pat ing the charming and homicidal interactions to be expected among Troubleshooting col- Teagues. After studying the pregenerated PCs, you'll see how it's done. Infact, as you design your own adventures you'll be better able to atch your own characters to your twisted dramatic needs, and certainly youl be better able to match your characters tothe strengths, peculiarities, and psychological instabilities of your players Second, using pregenerated PCs isa big help to novice players. They don’t have to master, the intricate details of a new character system right of the bat; instead, they start playing im- mediately. Paranoia sessions are ideally brief, intense, and fast-paced, and starting off a ses sion by rolling up characters is like stating a James Bond movie with twenty minutes of ‘Meet the Press’éstyle talking-heads exposition. For more experienced and ambitious players, cooperative character design is fun, Give your players a rough outline of their characters and, let them fil in the details. In any case, you'll still have to work up the distinctively paranoid refinements every character should have: * Some possession that is ambiguously valuable, partially incomprehensible, and eminently treasonous. ' Current secret society status — reputation within the group, standing orders, special in- teress, etc ‘Tidbits about other characters — vague rumors, fragmentary evidence, puzzling obser- vations — that foment distrust and provide tan- 
talizing hearsay evidence of treason, (Note: This is not always necessary — sometimes throwing Complete strangers together works just fine.) 
Gaps ad Seating Order Get in the habit of going around the table from left to right when players declare their actions, Get a quick statement from the frst, character, go immediately to the person next to him, and s0 on — bang, bang, bang, No stalling, no going back for second thoughts, ‘no interrupting with out-of-order comments. ‘This keeps things rolling along and maintains «a subtle stranglehold on the players’ sense of tree will 
‘A Paranola gamemaster in action 
There are two popular methods of ordering the seating of the players: * In ascending order of moxie score: this way the dumbest character acts first — without the tempering guidance and consultation of more sensible characters — a good way to encourage inresponsible, ill-considered, and entertaining actions.

ae Introducing Characters Before the session begins, have each player introduce is character tothe rs of the group. Keep intros short and to the point, Limit the comments to detail like: Name and security clearance: A great cheap trick is to scribble the character's full 
ame on a piece of paper and tuck it in a pocket or pin it on sot looks like the iden- tiieation strip on a military uniform. ‘A well-designed character has one oF two distinctive personality tats. A black marketeer may always seem to be well-equipped. A goon 
may absently fidget with a pair of brass 
knuckles, A mad scientist may constantly 
tinker with a widget. A heavily-crugged citizen 
‘may occasionally wander off into walls jabber- ing to himself. A marksman may regularly 
clean and polith bis weapon. With beginners, you might introduce the characters yours, giving the players a model of brevity, detail, and wit that they can follow: 
‘when they introduce their own characters ater they become more experienced 
Private Briefings 
‘After characters have been handed out and introduced, take the players aside one by one and give them some special tidbits — un- substantiated rumors about other player characters; vague hints from obscure inform: ants; secret society tips, warnings, of missions; ‘odd notes about strange happenings in Alpha ‘Complex; and so on, The point is to give the
